dialogue.utterance: EMS Unit 3 responding for breathing problems in [CITY].
Hello sir, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing for the last two days, and it's getting worse.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me what day it is today?
Saturday.
Where are you right now, sir?
My bedroom, second floor. I just called from here.
Good. Do you know your name, sir?
I'm Robert.
We'll need to move you to a safer location soon. Can you scoot to the stairchair with me, okay, a bit at a time, just so we can be safe if needed?
Fine, fine, I can manage that.
Your airway is open and self-maintained, which is good.
We're going to listen to your breathing-and it might sound like you're using some extra help to breathe. Okay with that?
Respirations are 26, adequate in depth, a bit labored, with wheezing in the upper lobes.
I hear some minimal wheezing during expiration. Can you take a quick, shallow breath for us before we finish up?
Deep breath...no, I can't do that.
That's fine. On a normal day, how would you describe your breathing?
Let's check something for us. What color would you say your skin is generally?
It's normal.
Radial pulse is 105 and regular.
Respirations are 26.
Blood pressure is 94 over 57.
SpO2 is 98% on room air.
Monitor shows sinus tachycardia, right bundle branch block. Medically reported to have 20% ejection fraction by spouse.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me about any other symptoms you've been experiencing besides your breathing difficulties?
No chest pain, but I have been short of breath on a 3-4 word basis.
You mention shortness of breath but no recent fevers, chills, body aches-have you had any recent travel or close contact with someone who was sick?
No, nothing like that, and I've felt like this once before a year ago.
Are you allergic to any medications, sir?
I react to morphine.
What medications do you usually take daily, if any?
I use my inhaler when needed, but it didn't help today.
What medical issues do you have that we need to know about?
I have COPD, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol.
He had his foot amputated recently due to peripheral vascular disease, and it healed well.
When was the last time you had something to eat or drink, Mr. Smith?
I had something at around 3 p.m.
You mentioned no chest discomfort. What about any pain or pressure anywhere else?
No pain anywhere else, just short of breath.
Skin is warm and dry with no rashes or lacerations.
No petechiae, bruising, or scars.
Pupils are equal and reactive, and appear round and symmetrical (PERRL).
No neck tenderness or masses reported-nothing found per the ePCR.
Taking a closer listen to your lungs now, sir-yes, wheezes are still present, and your lung sounds are diminished overall.
Before we do anything, do you mind if we hook you up to a monitor to keep track of your heart?
Sure, whatever will help is fine with me.
We'll now attach a 12-lead EKG. Can you lie back and not move, just for a moment?
We're running a 4-lead ECG monitor now as part of our cardiac protocol. Sir, we found you alert and oriented with no apparent distress-GCS of 15 (E4 V5 M6).
I will now give you a dose of terbutaline to help open up your breathing. Let me check things before I start.
Reading medication label...dose is 0.25mg via subcutaneous.
I'm using aseptic technique, pulling the plunger to ensure no blood is aspirated.
The medication is administered now-here's a bandaid applied over the site.
Respirations are 16 and less labored at this point.
Blood pressure is 128 over 83.
We are now giving 10mg of dexamethasone intravenously after a two-provider cross-check.
I've just started an 18G IV at your arm for future medication access.
Per ePCR, no glucose testing was done during transport.
A second 12-lead EKG just completed, findings consistent with right bundle branch block and sinus tachycardia.
It's time to move you to the stretcher. You will need a five-point seatbelt for transport.
Mr. Smith, are you on any home oxygen or equipment you need to bring with us?
No oxygen at home, just the rescue inhaler.
You are currently AAOx4, so mentally sharp.
I'll secure you on the stretcher now-whatever position feels best. Use a five-point seatbelt.
You are being placed on a RAA stretcher for transport.
Alright sir, we're all set for transport-any preference on where you'd like to be taken?
[COMPANY_NAME] is fine for me.
Got it. My partner will call ahead now for you.
Bed rails are being raised and you'll be placed on the ED stretcher safely.

Care is being turned over to the ER team. Vital signs prior to arrival: pulse 108, respirations 16, BP 128/83, SpO2 97, EKG with right bundle branch block and sinus tachycardia. Per ePCR, he used CPAP with relief previously and has no known PE or pulmonary edema.

ER on standby, we just picked up a 70-year-old with COPD exacerbation and respiratory disorder unspecified; vital signs were stable, we gave meds per protocol, and EKGs showed right bundle branch block and sinus tachycardia. We have our PPE: N95, eye protection, and gloves applied.
Administering 10cc NS flush through IV line.
Placed patient on 2LPM nasal cannula for comfort per request.
Per the vitals and assessments, we're ready to move whenever you are.
Five-point seatbelt resecured prior to initiating transport.
Ongoing vital monitoring during transport and enroute care.
Patient scooted from chair to stairchair and eventually stretcher via short steel stair walk.
Handing over a 70-year-old male with COPD, no chest pain, prior foot amputation due to PVD-no complications.
EMS assessments included respiratory disorder unspecified, COPD exacerbation. Oxygen at 2LPM nasal cannula.
